server\n\nimport (\n\t\"fmt\"\n\t\"net/http\"\n\t\"net/http/httptest\"\n\t\"strings\"\n\t\"testing\"\n)\n\n// =============================================================================\n// HTTP STATUS CODE VALIDATION HELPERS\n// =============================================================================\n// This module provides comprehensive HTTP status code validation utilities\n// for error response testing. It supports single status codes, multiple allowed\n// status codes, and flexible validation patterns.\n//\n// Functions:\n// - ValidateStatusCode: Validate single expected status code\n// - ValidateStatusCodeAny: Validate against multiple allowed status codes\n// - ValidateStatusCodeRange: Validate status code is within range\n// - CheckStatusCode: Non-asserting version that returns boolean\n// - AssertStatusCode: Flexible validation supporting both assertion and boolean modes\n// - AssertStatusCodeAny: Flexible multi-code validation with assertion mode\n// - StatusCodeMatchResult: Detailed validation result with error context\n// - GetStatusCodeDescription: Get human-readable status code description\n// =============================================================================\n\n// StatusCodeMatchResult contains detailed information about a status code validation.\n//\n// This type provides comprehensive error context for debugging validation failures,\n// including the expected and actual status codes with their descriptions, the\n// response object context, and a formatted error message.\ntype StatusCodeMatchResult struct {\n\t// Match indicates whether the status code matched successfully\n\tMatch bool\n\n\t// Expected is the status code that was expected\n\tExpected int\n\n\t// Actual is the status code that was received\n\tActual int\n\n\t// ExpectedDescription is the human-readable description of the expected status code\n\tExpectedDescription string\n\n\t// ActualDescription is the human-readable description of the actual status code\n\tActualDescription string\n\n\t// ResponseContext contains information about the response object\n\tResponseContext string\n\n\t// Error is a formatted error message describing the mismatch\n\tError string\n}\n\n// String returns a human-readable representation of the validation result.\nfunc (r StatusCodeMatchResult) String() string {\n\tif r.Match {\n\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"Status code match: %d (%s)\", r.Actual, r.ActualDescription)\n\t}\n\treturn r.Error\n}\n\n// AssertStatusCode validates status code with flexible assertion mode.\n//\n// This function supports both boolean-only mode (for conditional logic) and\n// assertion mode (with descriptive error messages). When assertMode is true\n// and validation fails, it returns a detailed error message showing expected\n// vs actual values along with response context.\n//\n// Parameters:\n//   - response: The HTTP response to validate (*httptest.ResponseRecorder or *http.Response)\n//   - expectedCode: The expected HTTP status code\n//   - assertMode: If true, return detailed error message on failure; if false, return bool only\n//\n// Returns:\n//   - StatusCodeMatchResult with detailed validation information\n//\n// Example (boolean mode):\n//   result := AssertStatusCode(w, 200, false)\n//   if !result.Match {\n//       // Handle non-200 case\n//   }\n//\n// Example (assertion mode):\n//   result := AssertStatusCode(w, 404, true)\n//   if !result.Match {\n//       t.Error(result.Error)  // Full error with expected vs actual\n//   }\nfunc AssertStatusCode(response interface{}, expectedCode int, assertMode bool) StatusCodeMatchResult {\n\tactualCode := getStatusCode(response)\n\n\t// Build response context for error messages\n\tresponseContext := getStatusCodeResponseContext(response)\n\n\t// Check if status codes match\n\tmatches := actualCode == expectedCode\n\n\tif matches {\n\t\treturn StatusCodeMatchResult{\n\t\t\tMatch:              true,\n\t\t\tExpected:           expectedCode,\n\t\t\tActual:             actualCode,\n\t\t\tExpectedDescription: GetStatusCodeDescription(expectedCode),\n\t\t\tActualDescription:   GetStatusCodeDescription(actualCode),\n\t\t\tResponseContext:     responseContext,\n\t\t\tError:              \"\",\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t// Build detailed error message\n\terrorMsg := buildStatusCodeMismatchError(\n\t\texpectedCode,\n\t\tactualCode,\n\t\tresponseContext,\n\t)\n\n\treturn StatusCodeMatchResult{\n\t\tMatch:              false,\n\t\tExpected:           expectedCode,\n\t\tActual:             actualCode,\n\t\tExpectedDescription: GetStatusCodeDescription(expectedCode),\n\t\tActualDescription:   GetStatusCodeDescription(actualCode),\n\t\tResponseContext:     responseContext,\n\t\tError:              errorMsg,\n\t}\n}\n\n// AssertStatusCodeAny validates status code against multiple allowed codes.\n//\n// This function is similar to AssertStatusCode but accepts multiple allowed\n// status codes. Returns detailed error information when validation fails,\n// including the full list of allowed status codes.\n//\n// Parameters:\n//   - response: The HTTP response to validate\n//   - allowedCodes: Slice of acceptable status codes\n//   - assertMode: If true, return detailed error message on failure\n//\n// Returns:\n//   - StatusCodeMatchResult with detailed validation information\n//\n// Example:\n//   result := AssertStatusCodeAny(w, []int{200, 201, 204}, true)\n//   if !result.Match {\n//       t.Error(result.Error)\n//   }\nfunc AssertStatusCodeAny(response interface{}, allowedCodes []int, assertMode bool) StatusCodeMatchResult {\n\tif len(allowedCodes) == 0 {\n\t\treturn StatusCodeMatchResult{\n\t\t\tMatch:              false,\n\t\t\tExpected:           0,\n\t\t\tActual:             0,\n\t\t\tExpectedDescription: \"\",\n\t\t\tActualDescription:   \"\",\n\t\t\tResponseContext:     getStatusCodeResponseContext(response),\n\t\t\tError:              \"AssertStatusCodeAny: allowedCodes cannot be empty\",\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tactualCode := getStatusCode(response)\n\tresponseContext := getStatusCodeResponseContext(response)\n\n\t// Check if actual code matches any in the allowed list\n\tfor _, allowedCode := range allowedCodes {\n\t\tif actualCode == allowedCode {\n\t\t\tallowedDesc := formatCodeList(allowedCodes)\n\t\t\treturn StatusCodeMatchResult{\n\t\t\t\tMatch:              true,\n\t\t\t\tExpected:           actualCode,\n\t\t\t\tActual:             actualCode,\n\t\t\t\tExpectedDescription: allowedDesc,\n\t\t\t\tActualDescription:   GetStatusCodeDescription(actualCode),\n\t\t\t\tResponseContext:     responseContext,\n\t\t\t\tError:              \"\",\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t// Build helpful error message showing what was allowed vs. what was received\n\tallowedDesc := formatCodeList(allowedCodes)\n\terrorMsg := buildStatusCodeMismatchErrorAny(\n\t\tallowedDesc,\n\t\tactualCode,\n\t\tresponseContext,\n\t)\n\n\treturn StatusCodeMatchResult{\n\t\tMatch:              false,\n\t\tExpected:           0,\n\t\tActual:             actualCode,\n\t\tExpectedDescription: allowedDesc,\n\t\tActualDescription:   GetStatusCodeDescription(actualCode),\n\t\tResponseContext:     responseContext,\n\t\tError:              errorMsg,\n\t}\n}\n\n// buildStatusCodeMismatchError creates a detailed error message for status code mismatches.\n//\n// This helper builds a comprehensive error message that includes:\n// - The expected status code with description\n// - The actual status code with description\n// - Response object context (type and status if available)\nfunc buildStatusCodeMismatchError(expected, actual int, responseContext string) string {\n\tvar b strings.Builder\n\n\tb.WriteString(\"Status code mismatch:\\n\")\n\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Expected: %d (%s)\\n\", expected, GetStatusCodeDescription(expected)))\n\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Actual:   %d (%s)\\n\", actual, GetStatusCodeDescription(actual)))\n\n\tif responseContext != \"\" {\n\t\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Context:  %s\\n\", responseContext))\n\t}\n\n\treturn b.String()\n}\n\n// buildStatusCodeMismatchErrorAny creates a detailed error message for multi-code mismatches.\n//\n// This helper builds a comprehensive error message when validating against multiple\n// allowed status codes, showing all allowed codes vs the actual received code.\nfunc buildStatusCodeMismatchErrorAny(allowedList string, actual int, responseContext string) string {\n\tvar b strings.Builder\n\n\tb.WriteString(\"Status code mismatch:\\n\")\n\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Expected: one of [%s]\\n\", allowedList))\n\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Actual:   %d (%s)\\n\", actual, GetStatusCodeDescription(actual)))\n\n\tif responseContext != \"\" {\n\t\tb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"  Context:  %s\\n\", responseContext))\n\t}\n\n\treturn b.String()\n}\n\n// getStatusCodeResponseContext extracts contextual information about the response object.\n//\n// This helper builds a string description of the response object for error messages.\n// For status code validation, the context includes the response type.\nfunc getStatusCodeResponseContext(response interface{}) string {\n\tswitch response.(type) {\n\tcase *httptest.ResponseRecorder:\n\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"httptest.ResponseRecorder\")\n\tcase *http.Response:\n\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"http.Response\")\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"unknown type: %T\", response)\n\t}\n}\n\n// formatCodeList formats a list of status codes with descriptions for error messages.\n//\n// This helper creates a readable string representation of multiple allowed codes,\n// including their descriptions for better debugging.\nfunc formatCodeList(codes []int) string {\n\tvar parts []string\n\tfor _, code := range codes {\n\t\tparts = append(parts, fmt.Sprintf(\"%d (%s)\", code, GetStatusCodeDescription(code)))\n\t}\n\treturn strings.Join(parts, \", \")\n}\n\n// ValidateStatusCode validates that the HTTP response has the expected status code.\n//\n// This is the basic status code validation helper that checks if a response\n// matches a single expected status code. It provides clear error messages\n// indicating what was expected vs. what was received.\n//\n// Parameters:\n//   - t: The testing instance for assertions\n//   - response: The HTTP response to validate (can be *httptest.ResponseRecorder or *http.Response)\n//   - expectedCode: The expected HTTP status code (e.g., 200, 404, 500)\n//\n// Example:\n//   ValidateStatusCode(t, w, 404)\nfunc ValidateStatusCode(t *testing.T, response interface{}, expectedCode int) {\n\tt.Helper()\n\n\tresult := AssertStatusCode(response, expectedCode, true)\n\n\tif !result.Match {\n\t\tt.Errorf(\"Status code validation failed:\\n  Expected: %d (%s)\\n  Actual:   %d (%s)\\n  Context:  %s\",\n\t\t\tresult.Expected, result.ExpectedDescription,\n\t\t\tresult.Actual, result.ActualDescription,\n\t\t\tresult.ResponseContext)\n\t}\n}\n\n// ValidateStatusCodeAny validates that the HTTP response has one of the allowed status codes.\n//\n// This helper is useful when multiple status codes are acceptable for a given scenario.\n// For example, when testing authentication, you might accept both 403 (Forbidden)\n// and 401 (Unauthorized) depending on the authentication method.\n//\n// Parameters:\n//   - t: The testing instance for assertions\n//   - response: The HTTP response to validate (can be *httptest.ResponseRecorder or *http.Response)\n//   - allowedCodes: Slice of acceptable HTTP status codes\n//\n// Example:\n//   ValidateStatusCodeAny(t, w, []int{200, 201, 204})\nfunc ValidateStatusCodeAny(t *testing.T, response interface{}, allowedCodes []int) {\n\tt.Helper()\n\n\tif len(allowedCodes) == 0 {\n\t\tt.Fatal(\"ValidateStatusCodeAny: allowedCodes cannot be empty\")\n\t\treturn\n\t}\n\n\tresult := AssertStatusCodeAny(response, allowedCodes, true)\n\n\tif !result.Match {\n\t\tt.Errorf(\"Status code validation failed:\\n  Expected: one of [%s]\\n  Actual:   %d (%s)\\n  Context:  %s\",\n\t\t\tresult.ExpectedDescription, result.Actual, result.ActualDescription, result.ResponseContext)\n\t}\n}\n\n// ValidateStatusCodeRange validates that the HTTP response status code falls within a range.\n//\n// This helper is useful for validating status codes by category, such as:\n// - 2xx for success responses (200-299)\n// - 4xx for client errors (400-499)\n// - 5xx for server errors (500-599)\n//\n// Parameters:\n//   - t: The testing instance for assertions\n//   - response: The HTTP response to validate\n//   - minCode: Minimum acceptable status code (inclusive)\n//   - maxCode: Maximum acceptable status code (inclusive)\n//\n// Example:\n//   // Validate 2xx success response\n//   ValidateStatusCodeRange(t, w, 200, 299)\n//\n//   // Validate 4xx client error\n//   ValidateStatusCodeRange(t, w, 400, 499)\nfunc ValidateStatusCodeRange(t *testing.T, response interface{}, minCode, maxCode int) {\n\tt.Helper()\n\n\tif minCode > maxCode {\n\t\tt.Fatalf(\"ValidateStatusCodeRange: minCode (%d) cannot be greater than maxCode (%d)\", minCode, maxCode)\n\t}\n\n\tactualCode := getStatusCode(response)\n\n\tif actualCode < minCode || actualCode > maxCode {\n\t\tt.Errorf(\"Expected HTTP status code in range [%d-%d] (%s), got %d (%s)\",\n\t\t\tminCode, maxCode, getRangeDescription(minCode, maxCode),\n\t\t\tactualCode, GetStatusCodeDescription(actualCode))\n\t}\n}\n\n// CheckStatusCode checks if the HTTP response has the expected status code without asserting.\n//\n// This non-asserting version returns a boolean, allowing for conditional logic\n// in tests. expectedCode: The expected HTTP status code\n//\n// Returns:\n//   - true if the response has the expected status code, false otherwise\n//\n// Example:\n//   if CheckStatusCode(w, 404) {\n//       // Patterns (error_test_patterns.go): Define expected behaviors for validation\n21\t// - HTTP Fixtures (this file): Create mock responses for testing\n22\t// Use fixtures when you need to simulate server responses, use patterns when validating them.\n23\t//\n24\t// When to use HTTP fixtures:\n25\t// - Mocking server error responses in integration tests\n26\t// - Creating expected responses for validation tests\n27\t// - Testing client error handling behavior\n28\t// - Building response samples for documentation\n29\t// - Writing tests that need complete HTTP response objects\n30\t//\n31\t// additional XML fields\n82\t\tAdditionalFields map[string]string\n83\t\n84\t\t// Headers contains additional HTTP headers\n85\t\tHeaders map[string]string\n86\t}\n87\t\n88\t// S3ErrorResponse XML samples for documentation\n108\t//   - Validating fixture XML structure\n109\tfunc (f *HTTPErrorFixture) ToXML() string {\n110\t\tresponse := S3ErrorResponse{\n111\t\t\tCode: f.AdditionalFields[\"RequestId\"]; ok {\n132\t\t\tresponse.RequestId = requestId\n133\t\t}\n134\t\n135\t\toutput, err := xml.MarshalIndent(response, \"\", \"  \")\n136\t\tif err != nil {\n137\t\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"<?xml version=\\\"1.0\\\" encoding=\\\"UTF-8\\\"?>\\n<Error>\\n  <Code>%s</Code>\\n  <Message>%s</Message>\\n</Error>\", f.ErrorCode, toResponseBody(body),\n163\t\t}\n164\t}\n165\t\n166\t// ToS3Error converts the fixture to an S3Error struct.\n167\t//\n168\t// Use this method when:\n169\t//   - Converting fixtures to S3Error for {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_cf6b722f35634ce886d6a49a","type":"tool_result","content":"1\tpackage server\n2\t\n3\t// This file demonstrates comprehensive usage of the predefined error patterns.\n4\t// It serves as documentation for how to use CommonErrorPatterns,\n5\t// AuthErrorPatterns, ClientErrorPatterns, and ServerErrorPatterns.\n6\t//\n7\t// # Error Pattern Usage Guide\n8\t//\n9\t// This file contains usage examples for all error pattern collections and\n10\t// helper functions. Each example demonstrates a specific use case with\n11\t// detailed explanations.\n12\t//\n13\t// # Pattern Collections\n14\t//\n15\t//   - CommonErrorPatterns: General-purpose patterns for common S3 errors\n16\t//   - AuthErrorPatterns: Authentication and authorization failures\n17\t//   - ClientErrorPatterns: 4xx client-side errors\n18\t//   - ServerErrorPatterns: 5xx server-side errors\n19\t//\n20\t// # Helper Functions\n21\t//\n22\t//   - PatternForCode(): Get pattern by error code\n23\t//   - PatternsForCategory(): Get all patterns for a category\n24\t//   - AllCommonPatterns(): Get all common patterns\n25\t//\n26\t// # Quick Reference\n27\t//\n28\t// 1. Use a pattern directly: CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n29\t// 2. Get pattern by code: PatternForCode(\"NoSuchKey\")\n30\t// 3. Get patterns by category: PatternsForCategory(CategoryAuth)\n31\t// 4. Get all common patterns: AllCommonPatterns()\n32\t\n33\timport (\n34\t\t\"fmt\"\n35\t\t\"time\"\n36\t)\n37\t\n38\t// =============================================================================\n39\t// SECTION 1: BASIC PATTERN ACCESS\n40\t// =============================================================================\n41\t// This section demonstrates how to access and use predefined error patterns.\n42\t// =============================================================================\n43\t\n44\t// ExampleUsage demonstrates how to use the predefined error patterns.\n45\t//\n46\t// This example shows the basic pattern access patterns available in the\n47\t// framework. Use this as a quick reference for getting started.\n48\tfunc ExampleUsage() {\n49\t\t// Access common error patterns directly\n50\t\tnotFoundPattern := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n51\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Pattern: %s\\n\", notFoundPattern.Name)\n52\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", notFoundPattern.ExpectedCode)\n53\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", notFoundPattern.ExpectedStatus)\n54\t\n55\t\t// Access authentication error patterns\n56\t\tauthPattern := AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader\n57\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Auth Pattern: %s\\n\", authPattern.Name)\n58\t\n59\t\t// Access client error patterns (4xx)\n60\t\tclientPattern := ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound\n61\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Client Pattern: %s\\n\", clientPattern.Name)\n62\t\n63\t\t// Access server error patterns (5xx)\n64\t\tserverPattern := ServerErrorPatterns.InternalError\n65\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Server Pattern: %s\\n\", serverPattern.Name)\n66\t}\n67\t\n68\t// =============================================================================\n69\t// SECTION 2: COMMON ERROR PATTERNS\n70\t// =============================================================================\n71\t// This section demonstrates usage of CommonErrorPatterns.\n72\t//\n73\t// CommonErrorPatterns provide ready-to-use configurations for the most\n74\t// frequently encountered S3 error scenarios. Use these when testing\n75\t// standard error responses.\n76\t//\n77\t// Available patterns:\n78\t//   - ResourceNotFound: 404 errors for missing objects\n79\t//   - AccessDenied: 403 errors for authentication failures\n80\t//   - InvalidRequest: 400 errors for malformed requests\n81\t//   - UnsupportedMediaType: 415 errors for content-type validation\n82\t//   - MethodNotAllowed: 405 errors for HTTP method restrictions\n83\t//   - InternalServerError: 500 errors for server failures\n84\t//   - SignatureMismatch: 403 errors for AWS signature validation\n85\t//   - RequestExpired: 403 errors for expired timestamps\n86\t// =============================================================================\n87\t\n88\t// ExampleCommonPatterns demonstrates usage of CommonErrorPatterns.\n89\t//\n90\t// Use this example when:\n91\t//   - Testing standard S3 error scenarios\n92\t//   - Validating error response structure\n93\t//   - Writing integration tests for error handling\n94\tfunc ExampleCommonPatterns() {\n95\t\t// Example 1: Resource not found pattern\n96\t\tnotFound := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n97\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", notFound.Name)\n98\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", notFound.ExpectedStatus)\n99\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", notFound.ExpectedCode)\n100\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Message: %s\\n\", notFound.ExpectedMessage)\n101\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", notFound.MaxResponseTime)\n102\t\n103\t\t// Example 2: Access denied pattern\n104\t\taccessDenied := CommonErrorPatterns.AccessDenied\n105\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", accessDenied.Name)\n106\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Category: %s\\n\", accessDenied.Category)\n107\t\n108\t\t// Example 3: Invalid request pattern\n109\t\tinvalidRequest := CommonErrorPatterns.InvalidRequest\n110\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", invalidRequest.Description)\n111\t\n112\t\t// Example 4: Iterating over all common patterns\n113\t\tallPatterns := AllCommonPatterns()\n114\t\tfor i, pattern := range allPatterns {\n115\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"%d. %s: %s (%d)\\n\",\n116\t\t\t\ti+1, pattern.Name, pattern.ExpectedCode, pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n117\t\t}\n118\t}\n119\t\n120\t// ExamplePatternInValidation demonstrates using patterns for validation.\n121\t//\n122\t// Use this example when:\n123\t//   - Validating actual error responses against patterns\n124\t//   - Writing test assertions for error responses\n125\t//   - Checking response times and message formats\n126\tfunc ExamplePatternInValidation() {\n127\t\t// Simulated validation logic\n128\t\tpattern := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n129\t\n130\t\t// Check HTTP status\n131\t\tactualStatus := 404\n132\t\tif actualStatus != pattern.ExpectedStatus {\n133\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Status mismatch: got %d, want %d\\n\",\n134\t\t\t\tactualStatus, pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n135\t\t}\n136\t\n137\t\t// Check error code\n138\t\tactualCode := \"NoSuchKey\"\n139\t\tif actualCode != pattern.ExpectedCode {\n140\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Code mismatch: got %s, want %s\\n\",\n141\t\t\t\tactualCode, pattern.ExpectedCode)\n142\t\t}\n143\t\n144\t\t// Check message length\n145\t\tmessage := \"The specified key does not exist\"\n146\t\tif len(message) < pattern.MinMessageLength {\n147\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Message too short: %d chars, want at least %d\\n\",\n148\t\t\t\tlen(message), pattern.MinMessageLength)\n149\t\t}\n150\t\n151\t\t// Check response time\n152\t\tresponseTime := 150 * time.Millisecond\n153\t\tif responseTime > pattern.MaxResponseTime {\n154\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Response time too slow: %v, want < %v\\n\",\n155\t\t\t\tresponseTime, pattern.MaxResponseTime)\n156\t\t}\n157\t}\n158\t\n159\t// =============================================================================\n160\t// SECTION 3: AUTHENTICATION ERROR PATTERNS\n161\t// =============================================================================\n162\t// This section demonstrates usage of AuthErrorPatterns.\n163\t//\n164\t// AuthErrorPatterns provide configurations for all authentication failure\n165\t// scenarios. Use these when testing authentication, authorization, and\n166\t// AWS signature validation.\n167\t//\n168\t// Available patterns:\n169\t//   - MissingAuthHeader: No Authorization header present\n170\t//   - InvalidAccessKeyId: Invalid or non-existent access key\n171\t//   - SignatureDoesNotMatch: Incorrect signature calculation\n172\t//   - MissingDateHeader: Required date header is missing\n173\t//   - RequestExpired: Request timestamp is too old\n174\t//   - MalformedAuthHeader: Invalid Authorization header format\n175\t// =============================================================================\n176\t\n177\t// ExampleAuthPatterns demonstrates usage of AuthErrorPatterns.\n178\t//\n179\t// Use this example when:\n180\t//   - Testing authentication failure scenarios\n181\t//   - Validating AWS signature behavior\n182\t//   - Writing security-focused tests\n183\tfunc ExampleAuthPatterns() {\n184\t\t// Example 1: Missing authentication header\n185\t\tmissingAuth := AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader\n186\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", missingAuth.Name)\n187\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", missingAuth.ExpectedCode)\n188\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", missingAuth.ExpectedStatus)\n189\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", missingAuth.MaxResponseTime)\n190\t\n191\t\t// Example 2: Invalid access key ID\n192\t\tinvalidKey := AuthErrorPatterns.InvalidAccessKeyId\n193\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", invalidKey.Description)\n194\t\n195\t\t// Example 3: Signature does not match\n196\t\tsigMismatch := AuthErrorPatterns.SignatureDoesNotMatch\n197\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Min Message Length: %d\\n\", sigMismatch.MinMessageLength)\n198\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Keywords: %v\\n\", sigMismatch.ExpectedKeywords)\n199\t\n200\t\t// Example 4: Request expired\n201\t\texpired := AuthErrorPatterns.RequestExpired\n202\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Category: %s\\n\", expired.Category)\n203\t\n204\t\t// Example 5: Get all auth patterns\n205\t\tauthPatterns := PatternsForCategory(CategoryAuth)\n206\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Total auth patterns: %d\\n\", len(authPatterns))\n207\t}\n208\t\n209\t// ExampleAuthInTesting demonstrates using auth patterns in test scenarios.\n210\t//\n211\t// Use this example when:\n212\t//   - Writing table-driven tests for authentication\n213\t//   - Creating comprehensive auth test suites\n214\t//   - Testing multiple auth failure scenarios\n215\tfunc ExampleAuthInTesting() {\n216\t\t// Define auth test scenarios\n217\t\tauthScenarios := []struct {\n218\t\t\tname    string\n219\t\t\tpattern ErrorScenarioConfig\n220\t\t\ttestURL string\n221\t\t}{\n222\t\t\t{\n223\t\t\t\tname:    \"Missing Auth Header\",\n224\t\t\t\tpattern: AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader,\n225\t\t\t\ttestURL: \"/test-bucket/key\",\n226\t\t\t},\n227\t\t\t{\n228\t\t\t\tname:    \"Invalid Access AuthErrorPatterns.SignatureDoesNotMatch,\n235\t\t\t\ttestURL: \"/test-bucket/key\",\n236\t\t\t},\n237\t\t\t{\n238\t\t\t\tname:    \"Request Expired\",\n239\t\t\t\tpattern: AuthErrorPatterns.RequestExpired,\n240\t\t\t\ttestURL: \"/test-bucket/key\",\n241\t\t\t},\n242\t\t}\n243\t\n244\t\t// Simulate running tests\n245\t\tfor _, scenario := range authScenarios {\n246\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Test: %s\\n\", scenario.name)\n247\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Pattern: %s\\n\", scenario.pattern.Name)\n248\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Expected Status: %d\\n\", scenario.pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n249\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Test URL: %s\\n\", scenario.testURL)\n250\t\t}\n251\t}\n252\t\n253\t// =============================================================================\n254\t// SECTION 4: CLIENT ERROR PATTERNS (4xx)\n255\t// =============================================================================\n256\t// This section demonstrates usage of ClientErrorPatterns.\n257\t//\n258\t// ClientErrorPatterns provide configurations for 4xx client-side errors.\n259\t// These are errors where the client made an invalid request and should\n260\t// correct their request before retrying.\n261\t//\n262\t// Available patterns:\n263\t//   - BadRequest: Generic 400 Bad Request errors\n264\t//   - NotFound: 404 Not Found errors\n265\t//   - MethodNotAllowed: 405 Method Not Allowed errors\n266\t//   - UnsupportedMediaType: 415 Unsupported Media Type errors\n267\t// =============================================================================\n268\t\n269\t// ExampleClientPatterns demonstrates usage of ClientErrorPatterns.\n270\t//\n271\t// Use this example when:\n272\t//   - Testing 4xx client error responses\n273\t//   - Validating request validation logic\n274\t//   - Testing API contract compliance\n275\tfunc ExampleClientPatterns() {\n276\t\t// Example 1: Bad request pattern\n277\t\tbadRequest := ClientErrorPatterns.BadRequest\n278\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", badRequest.Name)\n279\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", badRequest.ExpectedStatus)\n280\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", badRequest.ExpectedCode)\n281\t\n282\t\t// Example 2: Not found pattern (reuses CommonErrorPatterns)\n283\t\tnotFound := ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound\n284\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", notFound.Name)\n285\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", notFound.Description)\n286\t\n287\t\t// Example 3: Method not allowed pattern\n288\t\tmethodNotAllowed := ClientErrorPatterns.MethodNotAllowed\n289\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Message: %s\\n\", methodNotAllowed.ExpectedMessage)\n290\t\n291\t\t// Example 4: Unsupported media type pattern\n292\t\tunsupportedMedia := ClientErrorPatterns.UnsupportedMediaType\n293\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", unsupportedMedia.MaxResponseTime)\n294\t}\n295\t\n296\t// ExampleClientErrorsInAPI demonstrates using client patterns for API testing.\n297\t//\n298\t// Use this example when:\n299\t//   - Testing API endpoint error handling\n300\t//   - Validating client error responses\n301\t//   - Writing API integration tests\n302\tfunc ExampleClientErrorsInAPI() {\n303\t\t// Define API error scenarios\n304\t\tapiScenarios := []struct {\n305\t\t\tmethod  string\n306\t\t\tpath    string\n307\t\t\tpattern ErrorScenarioConfig\n308\t\t}{\n309\t\t\t{\n310\t\t\t\tmethod:  \"POST\",\n311\t\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/file.txt\",\n312\t\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.MethodNotAllowed,\n313\t\t\t},\n314\t\t\t{\n315\t\t\t\tmethod:  \"GET\",\n316\t\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/nonexistent.txt\",\n317\t\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound,\n318\t\t\t},\n319\t\t\t{\n320\t\t\t\tmethod:  \"PUT\",\n321\t\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/file.txt\",\n322\t\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.UnsupportedMediaType,\n323\t\t\t},\n324\t\t}\n325\t\n326\t\t// Simulate API testing\n327\t\tfor _, scenario := range apiScenarios {\n328\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"API Test: %s %s\\n\", scenario.method, scenario.path)\n329\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Expected Error: %s (%d)\\n\",\n330\t\t\t\tscenario.pattern.ExpectedCode,\n331\t\t\t\tscenario.pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n332\t\t}\n333\t}\n334\t\n335\t// =============================================================================\n336\t// SECTION 5: SERVER ERROR PATTERNS (5xx)\n337\t// =============================================================================\n338\t// This section demonstrates usage of ServerErrorPatterns.\n339\t//\n340\t// ServerErrorPatterns provide configurations for 5xx server-side errors.\n341\t// These are errors where the server failed to fulfill a valid request.\n342\t//\n343\t// Available patterns:\n344\t//   - InternalError: 500 Internal Server Error\n345\t//   - ServiceUnavailable: 503 Service Unavailable\n346\t// =============================================================================\n347\t\n348\t// ExampleServerPatterns demonstrates usage of ServerErrorPatterns.\n349\t//\n350\t// Use this example when:\n351\t//   - Testing server error handling\n352\t//   - Verifying server failure responses\n353\t//   - Testing circuit breaker logic\n354\tfunc ExampleServerPatterns() {\n355\t\t// Example 1: Internal error pattern\n356\t\tinternalError := ServerErrorPatterns.InternalError\n357\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", internalError.Name)\n358\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", internalError.ExpectedStatus)\n359\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", internalError.ExpectedCode)\n360\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", internalError.MaxResponseTime)\n361\t\n362\t\t// Example 2: Service unavailable pattern\n363\t\tserviceUnavailable := ServerErrorPatterns.ServiceUnavailable\n364\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", serviceUnavailable.Name)\n365\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", serviceUnavailable.ExpectedStatus)\n366\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", serviceUnavailable.Description)\n367\t\n368\t\t// Example 3: Comparing server error patterns\n369\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Internal Error Time: %v\\n\", internalError.MaxResponseTime)\n370\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Service Unavailable Time: %v\\n\", serviceUnavailable.MaxResponseTime)\n371\t}\n372\t\n373\t// ExampleServerErrorsInResilience demonstrates using server patterns for resilience testing.\n374\t//\n375\t// Use this example when:\n376\t//   - Testing error recovery logic\n377\t//   - Verifying retry behavior\n378\t//   - Testing circuit breaker implementations\n379\tfunc ExampleServerErrorsInResilience() {\n380\t\t// Define resilience test scenarios\n381\t\tresilienceScenarios := []struct {\n382\t\t\tscenario string\n383\t\t\tpattern  ErrorScenarioConfig\n384\t\t\taction   string\n385\t\t}{\n386\t\t\t{\n387\t\t\t\tscenario: \"Database Failure\",\n388\t\t\t\tpattern:  ServerErrorPatterns.InternalError,\n389\t\t\t\taction:   \"Retry with exponential backoff\",\n390\t\t\t},\n391\t\t\t{\n392\t\t\t\tscenario: \"Maintenance Mode\",\n393\t\t\t\tpattern:  ServerErrorPatterns.ServiceUnavailable,\n394\t\t\t\taction:   \"Wait and retry\",\n395\t\t\t},\n396\t\t\t{\n397\t\t\t\tscenario: \"Overload\",\n398\t\t\t\tpattern:  ServerErrorPatterns.ServiceUnavailable,\n399\t\t\t\taction:   \"Circuit breaker open\",\n400\t\t\t},\n401\t\t}\n402\t\n403\t\t// Simulate resilience testing\n404\t\tfor _, scenario := range resilienceScenarios {\n405\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Scenario: %s\\n\", scenario.scenario)\n406\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Pattern: %s (%d)\\n\",\n407\t\t\t\tscenario.pattern.Name,\n408\t\t\t\tscenario.pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n409\t\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Action: %s\\n\", scenario.action)\n410\t\t}\n411\t}\n412\t\n413\t// =============================================================================\n414\t// SECTION 6: HELPER FUNCTIONS\n415\t// server\n\n// This file demonstrates comprehensive usage of the predefined error patterns.\n// It serves as documentation for how to use CommonErrorPatterns,\n// AuthErrorPatterns, ClientErrorPatterns, and ServerErrorPatterns.\n//\n// # Error Pattern Usage Guide\n//\n// This file contains usage examples for all error pattern collections and\n// helper functions. Each example demonstrates a specific use case with\n// detailed explanations.\n//\n// # Pattern Collections\n//\n//   - CommonErrorPatterns: General-purpose patterns for common S3 errors\n//   - AuthErrorPatterns: Authentication and authorization failures\n//   - ClientErrorPatterns: 4xx client-side errors\n//   - ServerErrorPatterns: 5xx server-side errors\n//\n// # Helper Functions\n//\n//   - PatternForCode(): Get pattern by error code\n//   - PatternsForCategory(): Get all patterns for a category\n//   - AllCommonPatterns(): Get all common patterns\n//\n// # Quick Reference\n//\n// 1. Use a pattern directly: CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n// 2. Get pattern by code: PatternForCode(\"NoSuchKey\")\n// 3. Get patterns by category: PatternsForCategory(CategoryAuth)\n// 4. Get all common patterns: AllCommonPatterns()\n\nimport (\n\t\"fmt\"\n\t\"time\"\n)\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SECTION 1: BASIC PATTERN ACCESS\n// =============================================================================\n// This section demonstrates how to access and use predefined error patterns.\n// =============================================================================\n\n// ExampleUsage demonstrates how to use the predefined error patterns.\n//\n// This example shows the basic pattern access patterns available in the\n// framework. Use this as a quick reference for getting started.\nfunc ExampleUsage() {\n\t// Access common error patterns directly\n\tnotFoundPattern := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Pattern: %s\\n\", notFoundPattern.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", notFoundPattern.ExpectedCode)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", notFoundPattern.ExpectedStatus)\n\n\t// Access authentication error patterns\n\tauthPattern := AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Auth Pattern: %s\\n\", authPattern.Name)\n\n\t// Access client error patterns (4xx)\n\tclientPattern := ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Client Pattern: %s\\n\", clientPattern.Name)\n\n\t// Access server error patterns (5xx)\n\tserverPattern := ServerErrorPatterns.InternalError\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Server Pattern: %s\\n\", serverPattern.Name)\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SECTION 2: COMMON ERROR PATTERNS\n// =============================================================================\n// This section demonstrates usage of CommonErrorPatterns.\n//\n// CommonErrorPatterns provide ready-to-use configurations for the most\n// frequently encountered S3 error scenarios. Use these when testing\n// standard error responses.\n//\n// Available patterns:\n//   - ResourceNotFound: 404 errors for missing objects\n//   - AccessDenied: 403 errors for authentication failures\n//   - InvalidRequest: 400 errors for malformed requests\n//   - UnsupportedMediaType: 415 errors for content-type validation\n//   - MethodNotAllowed: 405 errors for HTTP method restrictions\n//   - InternalServerError: 500 errors for server failures\n//   - SignatureMismatch: 403 errors for AWS signature validation\n//   - RequestExpired: 403 errors for expired timestamps\n// =============================================================================\n\n// ExampleCommonPatterns demonstrates usage of CommonErrorPatterns.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing standard S3 error scenarios\n//   - Validating error response structure\n//   - Writing integration tests for error handling\nfunc ExampleCommonPatterns() {\n\t// Example 1: Resource not found pattern\n\tnotFound := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", notFound.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", notFound.ExpectedStatus)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", notFound.ExpectedCode)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Message: %s\\n\", notFound.ExpectedMessage)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", notFound.MaxResponseTime)\n\n\t// Example 2: Access denied pattern\n\taccessDenied := CommonErrorPatterns.AccessDenied\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", accessDenied.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Category: %s\\n\", accessDenied.Category)\n\n\t// Example 3: Invalid request pattern\n\tinvalidRequest := CommonErrorPatterns.InvalidRequest\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", invalidRequest.Description)\n\n\t// Example 4: Iterating over all common patterns\n\tallPatterns := AllCommonPatterns()\n\tfor i, pattern := range allPatterns {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"%d. %s: %s (%d)\\n\",\n\t\t\ti+1, pattern.Name, pattern.ExpectedCode, pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n\t}\n}\n\n// ExamplePatternInValidation demonstrates using patterns for validation.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Validating actual error responses against patterns\n//   - Writing test assertions for error responses\n//   - Checking response times and message formats\nfunc ExamplePatternInValidation() {\n\t// Simulated validation logic\n\tpattern := CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n\n\t// Check HTTP status\n\tactualStatus := 404\n\tif actualStatus != pattern.ExpectedStatus {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Status mismatch: got %d, want %d\\n\",\n\t\t\tactualStatus, pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n\t}\n\n\t// Check error code\n\tactualCode := \"NoSuchKey\"\n\tif actualCode != pattern.ExpectedCode {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Code mismatch: got %s, want %s\\n\",\n\t\t\tactualCode, pattern.ExpectedCode)\n\t}\n\n\t// Check message length\n\tmessage := \"The specified key does not exist\"\n\tif len(message) < pattern.MinMessageLength {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Message too short: %d chars, want at least %d\\n\",\n\t\t\tlen(message), pattern.MinMessageLength)\n\t}\n\n\t// Check response time\n\tresponseTime := 150 * time.Millisecond\n\tif responseTime > pattern.MaxResponseTime {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Response time too slow: %v, want < %v\\n\",\n\t\t\tresponseTime, pattern.MaxResponseTime)\n\t}\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SECTION 3: AUTHENTICATION ERROR PATTERNS\n// =============================================================================\n// This section demonstrates usage of AuthErrorPatterns.\n//\n// AuthErrorPatterns provide configurations for all authentication failure\n// scenarios. Use these when testing authentication, authorization, and\n// AWS signature validation.\n//\n// Available patterns:\n//   - MissingAuthHeader: No Authorization header present\n//   - InvalidAccessKeyId: Invalid or non-existent access key\n//   - SignatureDoesNotMatch: Incorrect signature calculation\n//   - MissingDateHeader: Required date header is missing\n//   - RequestExpired: Request timestamp is too old\n//   - MalformedAuthHeader: Invalid Authorization header format\n// =============================================================================\n\n// ExampleAuthPatterns demonstrates usage of AuthErrorPatterns.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing authentication failure scenarios\n//   - Validating AWS signature behavior\n//   - Writing security-focused tests\nfunc ExampleAuthPatterns() {\n\t// Example 1: Missing authentication header\n\tmissingAuth := AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", missingAuth.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", missingAuth.ExpectedCode)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", missingAuth.ExpectedStatus)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", missingAuth.MaxResponseTime)\n\n\t// Example 2: Invalid access key ID\n\tinvalidKey := AuthErrorPatterns.InvalidAccessKeyId\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", invalidKey.Description)\n\n\t// Example 3: Signature does not match\n\tsigMismatch := AuthErrorPatterns.SignatureDoesNotMatch\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Min Message Length: %d\\n\", sigMismatch.MinMessageLength)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Keywords: %v\\n\", sigMismatch.ExpectedKeywords)\n\n\t// Example 4: Request expired\n\texpired := AuthErrorPatterns.RequestExpired\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Category: %s\\n\", expired.Category)\n\n\t// Example 5: Get all auth patterns\n\tauthPatterns := PatternsForCategory(CategoryAuth)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Total auth patterns: %d\\n\", len(authPatterns))\n}\n\n// ExampleAuthInTesting demonstrates using auth patterns in test scenarios.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Writing table-driven tests for authentication\n//   - Creating comprehensive auth test suites\n//   - Testing multiple auth failure scenarios\nfunc ExampleAuthInTesting() {\n\t// Define auth test scenarios\n\tauthScenarios := []struct {\n\t\tname    string\n\t\tpattern ErrorScenarioConfig\n\t\ttestURL string\n\t}{\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tname:    \"Missing Auth Header\",\n\t\t\tpattern: AuthErrorPatterns.MissingAuthHeader,\n\t\t\ttestURL: \"/test-bucket/key\",\n\t\t},\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tname:    \"Invalid Access \"/test-bucket/key\",\n\t\t},\n\t}\n\n\t// Simulate running tests\n\tfor _, scenario := range authScenarios {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"Test: %s\\n\", scenario.name)\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Pattern: %s\\n\", scenario.pattern.Name)\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Expected Status: %d\\n\", scenario.pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Test URL: %s\\n\", scenario.testURL)\n\t}\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SECTION 4: CLIENT ERROR PATTERNS (4xx)\n// =============================================================================\n// This section demonstrates usage of ClientErrorPatterns.\n//\n// ClientErrorPatterns provide configurations for 4xx client-side errors.\n// These are errors where the client made an invalid request and should\n// correct their request before retrying.\n//\n// Available patterns:\n//   - BadRequest: Generic 400 Bad Request errors\n//   - NotFound: 404 Not Found errors\n//   - MethodNotAllowed: 405 Method Not Allowed errors\n//   - UnsupportedMediaType: 415 Unsupported Media Type errors\n// =============================================================================\n\n// ExampleClientPatterns demonstrates usage of ClientErrorPatterns.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing 4xx client error responses\n//   - Validating request validation logic\n//   - Testing API contract compliance\nfunc ExampleClientPatterns() {\n\t// Example 1: Bad request pattern\n\tbadRequest := ClientErrorPatterns.BadRequest\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", badRequest.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", badRequest.ExpectedStatus)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", badRequest.ExpectedCode)\n\n\t// Example 2: Not found pattern (reuses CommonErrorPatterns)\n\tnotFound := ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", notFound.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", notFound.Description)\n\n\t// Example 3: Method not allowed pattern\n\tmethodNotAllowed := ClientErrorPatterns.MethodNotAllowed\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Message: %s\\n\", methodNotAllowed.ExpectedMessage)\n\n\t// Example 4: Unsupported media type pattern\n\tunsupportedMedia := ClientErrorPatterns.UnsupportedMediaType\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", unsupportedMedia.MaxResponseTime)\n}\n\n// ExampleClientErrorsInAPI demonstrates using client patterns for API testing.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing API endpoint error handling\n//   - Validating client error responses\n//   - Writing API integration tests\nfunc ExampleClientErrorsInAPI() {\n\t// Define API error scenarios\n\tapiScenarios := []struct {\n\t\tmethod  string\n\t\tpath    string\n\t\tpattern ErrorScenarioConfig\n\t}{\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tmethod:  \"POST\",\n\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/file.txt\",\n\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.MethodNotAllowed,\n\t\t},\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tmethod:  \"GET\",\n\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/nonexistent.txt\",\n\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.NotFound,\n\t\t},\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tmethod:  \"PUT\",\n\t\t\tpath:    \"/api/blobs/file.txt\",\n\t\t\tpattern: ClientErrorPatterns.UnsupportedMediaType,\n\t\t},\n\t}\n\n\t// Simulate API testing\n\tfor _, scenario := range apiScenarios {\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"API Test: %s %s\\n\", scenario.method, scenario.path)\n\t\tfmt.Printf(\"  Expected Error: %s (%d)\\n\",\n\t\t\tscenario.pattern.ExpectedCode,\n\t\t\tscenario.pattern.ExpectedStatus)\n\t}\n}\n\n// =============================================================================\n// SECTION 5: SERVER ERROR PATTERNS (5xx)\n// =============================================================================\n// This section demonstrates usage of ServerErrorPatterns.\n//\n// ServerErrorPatterns provide configurations for 5xx server-side errors.\n// These are errors where the server failed to fulfill a valid request.\n//\n// Available patterns:\n//   - InternalError: 500 Internal Server Error\n//   - ServiceUnavailable: 503 Service Unavailable\n// =============================================================================\n\n// ExampleServerPatterns demonstrates usage of ServerErrorPatterns.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing server error handling\n//   - Verifying server failure responses\n//   - Testing circuit breaker logic\nfunc ExampleServerPatterns() {\n\t// Example 1: Internal error pattern\n\tinternalError := ServerErrorPatterns.InternalError\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", internalError.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", internalError.ExpectedStatus)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Code: %s\\n\", internalError.ExpectedCode)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Max Response Time: %v\\n\", internalError.MaxResponseTime)\n\n\t// Example 2: Service unavailable pattern\n\tserviceUnavailable := ServerErrorPatterns.ServiceUnavailable\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Name: %s\\n\", serviceUnavailable.Name)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Expected Status: %d\\n\", serviceUnavailable.ExpectedStatus)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Description: %s\\n\", serviceUnavailable.Description)\n\n\t// Example 3: Comparing server error patterns\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Internal Error Time: %v\\n\", internalError.MaxResponseTime)\n\tfmt.Printf(\"Service Unavailable Time: %v\\n\", serviceUnavailable.MaxResponseTime)\n}\n\n// ExampleServerErrorsInResilience demonstrates using server patterns for resilience testing.\n//\n// Use this example when:\n//   - Testing error recovery logic\n//   - Verifying retry behavior\n//   - Testing circuit breaker implementations\nfunc
